male-60-asphalt.name: George Patterson
male-60-asphalt.variantDescription: Bladder cancer from PAH exposure in asphalt work

missedConsequences.Bladder Cancer: Delayed diagnosis = advanced stage = worse outcomes.

allDiagnoses.name: UTI
allDiagnoses.rationale: Painless gross hematuria needs cancer workup regardless.

male-60-asphalt.oldcartsNarrative: "Blood in my urine for 2 weeks. No pain, no burning. 35 years paving roads - asphalt work. Lots of tar fumes. Also did coke oven work earlier in career. Smoked 30 years but quit 10 years ago."

historyQuestions.answer: 35 years road paving (asphalt fumes) and coke oven work. High PAH exposure.

historyQuestions.text: What medications are you currently taking?
historyQuestions.reasoning: Medications can cause symptoms and affect treatment choices.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any medication allergies?
Do you have any other medical conditions?
historyQuestions.reasoning: Past medical history affects diagnosis and treatment.
Surgical history can explain symptoms and affect planning.

occupation_pah.answer: 35 years asphalt paving and coke oven work - high PAH (polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon) exposure
occupation_pah.relevance: PAH metabolites are excreted in urine and are bladder carcinogens.

physicalExam.finding: No palpable masses. No CVA tenderness. Normal prostate.

imaging.name: CT Urogram
imaging.result: 2.5 cm enhancing mass in bladder. No upper tract lesions.

imaging.text: What medications are you currently taking?
imaging.answer: Blood pressure medicine, I think lisinopril.
imaging.reasoning: Medications can cause or contribute to symptoms and affect treatment choices.

imaging.text: Do you have any medication allergies?
imaging.reasoning: Critical for safe prescribing and treatment planning.

imaging.text: Do you have any chronic medical conditions?
imaging.answer: High blood pressure, that's about it.
imaging.reasoning: Past medical history affects differential diagnosis and management.

imaging.text: Have you had any prior surgeries?
imaging.reasoning: Surgical history can explain symptoms and affect treatment options.

procedures.name: Cystoscopy with biopsy
procedures.result: Papillary mass at dome. Biopsy: high-grade urothelial carcinoma.

procedures.text: What medications are you currently taking?
procedures.answer: Blood pressure medicine, I think lisinopril.
procedures.reasoning: Medications can cause or contribute to symptoms and affect treatment choices.

procedures.text: Do you have any medication allergies?
procedures.reasoning: Critical for safe prescribing and treatment planning.

procedures.text: Do you have any chronic medical conditions?
procedures.answer: High blood pressure, that's about it.
procedures.reasoning: Past medical history affects differential diagnosis and management.

procedures.text: Have you had any prior surgeries?
procedures.reasoning: Surgical history can explain symptoms and affect treatment options.

actualDiagnosis.name: Occupational Bladder Cancer (Urothelial Carcinoma)
teachingPoints: Asphalt workers, coke oven workers, rubber workers at risk
Painless hematuria = cancer until proven otherwise

guidelineReferences.name: IARC Monographs on Carcinogens - Occupational Exposures
guidelineReferences.citation: International Agency for Research on Cancer
keyPoints: Occupational PAH exposure associated with bladder cancer
Rubber manufacturing, dye industry, asphalt work are high-risk
Latency period typically 15-40 years after first exposure

guidelineReferences.name: AUA Guideline: Non-Muscle Invasive Bladder Cancer
keyPoints: Urine cytology has high specificity for high-grade tumors
CT urography for upper tract evaluation
Occupational history should be documented for compensation

patientBackground.recentEvents: Patient was in usual state of health until symptoms began. No major recent life changes.
patientBackground.recentIllnesses: No significant recent illnesses other than this presentation.
patientBackground.contextualFactors: Dealing with typical life stressors. This illness is disrupting normal activities.
patientBackground.supportSystem: Has family and friends available for support. Not socially isolated.
patientBackground.healthBeliefs: Generally trusts medical advice. Wants to understand the reasoning behind recommendations.
patientBackground.previousERVisits: Uses healthcare appropriately. No excessive ER utilization.

questions.stem: Based on the clinical presentation, which is the MOST appropriate next step?

options.explanation: Empiric treatment before adequate evaluation risks missing the correct diagnosis and may cause harm.

options.text: Systematic history and focused physical examination
options.explanation: A targeted history and physical exam is the essential first step in clinical reasoning, guiding hypothesis generation and differential diagnosis.
Consultation may be appropriate later but should follow initial clinical assessment and diagnostic reasoning.
Diagnostic testing should be hypothesis-driven, not shotgun; targeted tests based on clinical reasoning yield better outcomes.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Systematic history and focused physical examination.
correct.deeperWhy: A targeted history and physical exam is the essential first step in clinical reasoning, guiding hypothesis generation and differential diagnosis.
correct.clinicalPearl: A targeted history and physical exam is the essential first step in clinical reasoning, guiding hypothesis generation and differential diagnosis.

questions.stem: Which cognitive bias poses the greatest risk in this clinical scenario?

options.explanation: Availability bias (overweighting recent experiences) is common but not the most frequent source of error in this scenario.
Premature closure — accepting a diagnosis before it is fully verified — is the single most common cognitive error in clinical reasoning across all settings.
Anchoring (fixating on initial impression) is a risk but can be mitigated by systematic differential generation.
Confirmation bias (seeking only supporting evidence) contributes to error but often follows from premature closure.

correct.deeperWhy: Premature closure — accepting a diagnosis before it is fully verified — is the single most common cognitive error in clinical reasoning across all settings.
correct.clinicalPearl: Premature closure — accepting a diagnosis before it is fully verified — is the single most common cognitive error in clinical reasoning across all settings.
